conclusion: 3G's model is more patient and quality-obsessed than its cost-cutter reputation. The pattern is one great business per fund, heavy house capital, operator-investors, young talent with real ownership, and long relationships with family businesses. Zero-based budgeting matters, but business quality matters more.
background: Alex Behring and Daniel Schwartz explain 3G Capital from the inside: Brazilian roots, one investment per fund, operator DNA, Burger King, Tim Hortons, Hunter Douglas, Skechers, and lessons from Kraft Heinz. Alex ran a large Latin American railroad at age 30; Daniel became CFO and CEO of Burger King in his early 30s. The episode is a detailed private-equity operating manual: concentrate because great businesses and great CEOs are rare, preserve downside, keep house capital high, develop talent early, centralize the what not the how, and use cost discipline as a tool for growth rather than a substitute for business quality.
important_facts: 3G's model is one investment per fund, with the firm and co-founders/partners as the largest investors in each deal. RBI has been held about 15 years and counting; Burger King returned roughly 25-30x multiple on capital. Investment implications in the summary are: QSR (Long) — RBI remains a royalty-like global franchise platform with 30,000+ restaurants versus roughly 12,000 when 3G bought Burger King, leaving international white space. 3G's reputation overweights cost cutting and underweights business quality. What do outsiders misunderstand? They spend most investment meetings deciding whether a business is truly good. At an RBI offsite, 10 pages covered costs and about 790 covered growth, operations, and new restaurants.

Hunter Douglas has a roughly $70B interior/exterior window coverings TAM, billions of custom permutations, ≈100-year family history, and delivery often within 1-2 weeks through scaled manufacturing and distribution. Skechers is the third-largest sneaker company by sneaker sales: about $9B versus Adidas around $14B; it is 99% footwear, two-thirds outside the US, has 5,000+ stores, and has been adding about $1B of sales per year. What do outsiders misunderstand? They spend most investment meetings deciding whether a business is truly good. mental_model.name: One Basket, Watch It Closely
mental_model.components: 3G's reputation overweights cost cutting and underweights business quality. They spend most investment meetings deciding whether a business is truly good. At an RBI offsite, 10 pages covered costs and about 790 covered growth, operations, and new restaurants. The best businesses own the end-customer relationship. Private label and big retailers can disintermediate CPG brands with customer concentration. Burger King, Tim Hortons, Hunter Douglas, and Skechers have more direct brand or distribution control. Young talent works only when ownership, mentorship, and stakes are real. Alex was given the railroad at 30, Daniel became Burger King CEO at 32, Josh Kobza became CFO at 26. They were surrounded by experienced operators and given meaningful economics and responsibility.
mental_model.application: 3G applies this framework by spending substantial diligence time on customer relationship durability, unit-level economics, and leadership bench depth before deals close. Post-close, they pair clear financial accountability with operator development and decentralized execution. The Burger King-to-RBI path, Hunter Douglas acquisition, and Skechers investment are presented as examples where business quality, global scale room, and governance alignment support multi-year compounding.

top_investment_implications.thesis: RBI remains a royalty-like global franchise platform with 30,000+ restaurants versus roughly 12,000 when 3G bought Burger King, leaving international white space.
Hunter Douglas offers a $70B window-coverings TAM, custom product complexity, scaled distribution and service, and energy-saving tailwinds that support 3G's quality filter.
Skechers is the third-largest sneaker company, with about $9B of sales, 5,000+ stores, no single hero SKU, and two-thirds of revenue outside the US.

golden_quotes: "Behring summarizes 3G's concentrated mandate: 'We have this luxury of only having to find one great business at a time.'"
"Their operating style is disciplined but not micromanaged: 'Centralize the what, not the how.'"
"On the cost-cutting caricature, the warning is explicit: 'I wouldn't recommend one of your listeners buy a lousy business with a big zero-based budgeting opportunity.'"
